Motivation
¶

Diabetes is, in fact, a global epidemic. Millions of individuals live with undiagnosed or poorly managed diabetes, leading to severe complications such as cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, blindness, and lower-limb amputation. Knowing and preventing more this disease is, indeed, a high important task.

Thus, before we try to create any machine learning model, we need to not only handle inconsistencies, cleanning or droping imbalanced classes, but most important to understand more about the relations between the features. After all that, then we could proceede to creating a machine learning classification algoritm.

1) Importing libraries¶

In order to create a machine learning model that can estimates whether a patient has or has not tendencies with diabetes, we need to import libraries that can handle machine learning and data analysis workflows.

  • Third party packages:
    • matplotlib: to customize and create our own graphs in order to understand the data;
    • seaborn: to help create more specific data graphs less easily;
    • numpy: to work and handle data more efficiently;
    • polars: for fast data manipulation;
    • scikit-learn: for data algoritms;
    • scipy: for statistic functions.;
  • Functions:
    • plots: to help create plots already determined;
    • statistics: to help calculate values more easy;

2) Reading dataset¶

2.1) Overview¶

The Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) is a health-related telephone survey that is collected annually by the Centers for Disease Control (CDC).

Each year, the survey collects responses from over 400,000 Americans on health-related risk behaviors, chronic health conditions, and the use of preventative services. It has been conducted every year since 1984. For this project, we will use a 2015 dataset available on Kaggle.

2.1.1) Understanding columns¶

The dataset was already been cleanned before, therefore, some columns also has already been transformed to the point where some categorical values are now or binary or integers expressing some ordering. Luckily for us, the Kaggle community already have had a discussion about the meaning of each columns.

After reading the forum, we could rewrite each parameter as:


Feature Type Meaning Values
Diabetes_012 Categorical Indicates if the patient has diabetes or pre-diabetes. 0 = No diabetes; 1 = Pre-diabetes; 2 = Diabetes
HighBP Categorical High blood pressure (as told by a health professional). 0 = No; 1 = Yes
HighChol Categorical High cholesterol (as told by a health professional). 0 = No; 1 = Yes
CholCheck Categorical Cholesterol check within the last 5 years. 0 = No; 1 = Yes
BMI Float Body Mass Index. Numerical value (e.g., 28.5)
Smoker Categorical Have smoked at least 100 cigarettes in your entire life. 0 = No; 1 = Yes
Stroke Categorical Ever told you had a stroke. 0 = No; 1 = Yes
HeartDiseaseorAttack Categorical Coronary heart disease (CHD) or myocardial infarction (MI). 0 = No; 1 = Yes
PhysActivity Categorical Physical activity in past 30 days (excluding job). 0 = No; 1 = Yes
Fruits Categorical Consume fruit 1 or more times per day. 0 = No; 1 = Yes
Veggies Categorical Consume vegetables 1 or more times per day. 0 = No; 1 = Yes
HvyAlcoholConsump Categorical Heavy drinkers (Men >14 drinks/week; Women >7 drinks/week). 0 = No; 1 = Yes
AnyHealthcare Categorical Have any kind of health care coverage. 0 = No; 1 = Yes
NoDocbcCost Categorical Was there a time in past 12 months you needed a doctor but could not see one because of cost? 0 = No; 1 = Yes
GenHlth Categorical Self-reported general health on a scale of 1-5. 1 = Excellent; 2 = Very good; 3 = Good; 4 = Fair; 5 = Poor
MentHlth Integer Number of days of poor mental health in the last 30 days. Scale 0 - 30
PhysHlth Integer Number of days of physical illness or injury in the last 30 days. Scale 0 - 30
DiffWalk Categorical Serious difficulty walking or climbing stairs. 0 = No; 1 = Yes
Sex Categorical Biological sex. 0 = Female; 1 = Male
Age Categorical 13-level age category (5-year increments). 1 = 18-24 up to 13 = 80 or older
Education Categorical Education level scale. 1 = Never attended school up to 6 = College graduate
Income Categorical Annual household income scale. 1 = <$10k up to 8 = >$75k

This is important to know because we are aiming to create a model that can return whether a patient has or has not tendencies with diabetes. Thus, this dictionary is importante because we can now specify each feature correctly.

2.2) Setting correct values¶

As we saw on top, mainly on those columns that represent a categorical non boolean feature, we need to replace preprocessing values in order to make our dataset more understandable. For those features that have boolean caracteristics, we are just going to replace to True and False.

For personal preference, we are also going to rename some columns in order to really have a more presentable dataset, even though that is not necessary.

3) Understanding dataset¶

In order to get a more understanding knowledge about the data that we are handling here, we need to check if there are, for example, outliers or any other patterns that could be used in our favor. Therefore, we are going to split this section according to the list bellow:

  1. Seeing imbalance: since DIABETES_LEVEL is the target feature, it could have minority values that needs to be handled properly;
  2. Correlations: checking if there are any kind or degree of correlation with our target feature (DIABETES_LEVEL);
  3. Importance Value: checking if there are any columns that have a high importance with target feature (DIABETES_LEVEL);
  4. Checking data normalization: checking if columns with number values have a normal distribution;
  5. Searching outliers: try to get outliers based again on columns with number values;

3.1) Seeing imbalance¶

As we can see, we have three diferents categories in DIABETES_LEVEL: no_diabetes, pre_diabetes and diabetes. In our case, we are trying to figure out if a certain person has or do not has diabetes.

3.1.1) Visualizing imbalance¶

Therefore, we will create a simple dual plot that shows us the percentage amoung those categories in the target column DIABETES_LEVEL.

3.1.2) Droping pre_diabetes registers¶

As we saw on top, we only have 1.83% (which only corresponds to 4.631 rows) of pre_diabetes in the entire dataset (with 253.680 rows). Even though we understand that pre_diabetes is a also a serious condition, we decided to drop those registers not only because of the minor size, but also because the main goal of this project is to find out if a person has or has not diabetes.

Estimated dataframe size: 2.762Mb
(249049, 22)

3.2) Correlations¶

Correlation in a dataset could cause redundancies, and it is bad for machine learning because some features could have, for example, its importance multiply by other similar features. Furthermore, we need to seek correlations in our dataset and find a way to avoid vicious behaviour in our future model.

3.2.1) Visualizing correlations¶

According to the interpretation of the module of index correlation bellow, we do not find a single value between the range [0.70, 1.00], with indicate us that all features are - in some way - independent. However, there are some cases where some features can reach [0.30, 0.50] or even [0.50, 0.70] range correlation, which needs in fact more exploration.

RANGE
CORRELATION LEVEL
0.00 - 0.10 inexistent
0.10 - 0.30 weak
0.30 - 0.50 moderate
0.50 - 0.70 high
0.70 - 1.00 very high

3.2.2) Handling strong correlation¶

3.2.2.A) Ploting correlation¶

3.2.2.A.a) DAYS_INJURY, HEALTH_LEVEL and WALK_DIFFICULTY

¶

Those features are the ones who have a similar and strong correlation, even though is not strong enough to assume that all features are the same. Since each feature has a specific dtype, we are force to use other plot than the standart scatter plot. Let's see how much each variable behave with each other.

As we saw on top, those features indeed have a positive correlation behaviour, even though it is not strong enough. Here is the overall interpretation:

  • WALK_DIFFICULTY and DAYS_INJURY: people that confirms been walking difficulty usually tell that has been injuried for a longer period of time;
  • HEALTH_LEVEL and DAYS_INJURY: people that evalute themselfs poorly also tell that has been injuried for a longer period of time;
  • WALK_DIFFICULTY and HEALTH_LEVEL: as people report feeling worse, the response of difficulty walking gradually increases.

3.2.2.A.b) EDUCATION_LEVEL and INCOME_RANGE

¶

Even though those features do not have a high correlation, in this particular case we have something that could cause some discomfort measuring: INCOME_RANGE. Since this feature could be considered a sensitive information for a lot of users, we can ignore this column.

As we saw on top, those features indeed have a positive correlation behaviour, even though it is not strong enough. Here is the overall interpretation:

  • EDUCATION_LEVEL and INCOME_RANGE: as people report high EDUCATION_LEVEL, the response of INCOME_RANGE gradually increases. For example, it becomes more easy to have high salaries in ranges where the education is graduate_completed.
3.2.2.B) Applying PCA¶

PCA is a method used to simplify a number of n features into a m (m < n) features. It is very helpfull not only to reduce the size of the data, but it also can avoid redundancy in features that may have a high correlation. However, in order to apply any PCA transformations, we must have to have at least a more comparable standard deviation between these variables.

shape: (1, 4)
statisticWALK_DIFFICULTYHEALTH_LEVELDAYS_INJURY
strf64f64f64
"std"0.3722541.0673088.681035

In order words, we should transform DAYS_INJURY in order to have a standard deviation similar to what we have found after reducing categories in HEALTH_LEVEL (1.067) and WALK_DIFFICULTY (0.372).

Therefore, we must have to transforme DAYS_INJURY using RobustScaler (10, 90) to have better and fair results using PCA:

3.2.2.C) Dropping columns¶

3.3) Importance Value¶

Another way to find features that have a high influence on the DIABETES_LEVEL target is to find for each feature the Importance Value (IV).


Luckily for us, there are not a single feature that surpass ranges where we could investigate any level of predictive power.

RANGE
Predictive Power
< 0.02 not useful for prediction
0.02 - 0.10 weak predictive power
0.10 - 0.30 medium predictive power
0.30 - 0.50 strong predictive power
> 0.50 suspiciously high
shape: (18, 2)
FEATUREIMPORTANCE

"DAYS_BAD_MENTAL_HEALTH"0.003631
"NO_DOCTOR_APPOINTMENT_BY_COST"0.002615
"CONSUME_ALCOOL"0.00237

3.4) Checking outliers¶

There are only three features with integer or float values: BODY_MASS, DAYS_BAD_MENTAL_HEALTH, PCA_HEALTH_&_INJURY_&_WALK_DIFFICULTY. For each column, we should plot a box plot not only in order to see if there are any outliers, but to check if those outliers are worth to drop it.

As we saw, outliers are in any features with integer ou float dtypes - however, not all features must be treated as the same. As we know, both DAYS_BAD_MENTAL_HEALTH and PCA_HEALTH_&_INJURY_&_WALK_DIFFICULTY are directly or indirectly limited to the range [0, 30] - which makes removing outliers semantically desnecessary since it cannot go any high than that. However, since there are also so many outliers related to BODY_MASS and - in this case - no guarantee of limits, we should remove them.

In other hand, removing outliers related to BODY_MASS should not be a good ideia because, as we saw previously, BODY_MASS and DIABETES_LEVEL have a positive correlation and also - if we drop these outliers - we will remove more than 10% of diabetes cases (which is a minority and also our main goal in this project).

Therefore, in order to hide from our model outlier values, we are going to group BODY_MASS based on certain ranges.

4) Sumarizing cleanning stage¶

In order to have a fully automate way for what we have done so far related to the cleanning process, we will apply a pipeline. Also, we are going to save this pipeline process into a archive .joblib where our future model can make better previsions eventually.

ColumnTransformer(remainder='passthrough',
                  transformers=[('pca_transformer',
                                 Pipeline(steps=[('injury_days_transformer',
                                                  ColumnTransformer(remainder='passthrough',
                                                                    transformers=[('injury_days_pipeline_robust_scaler',
                                                                                   RobustScaler(quantile_range=(10,
                                                                                                                90)),
                                                                                   ['DAYS_INJURY']),
                                                                                  ('health_level_pipeline_ordinal_encoder',
                                                                                   OrdinalEncoder(categories=[['poor',
                                                                                                               'fair',
                                                                                                               '...
                                                             'college_graduate_completed']]),
                                 ['EDUCATION_LEVEL']),
                                ('sex_one_hot_encoder', OrdinalEncoder(),
                                 ['SEX']),
                                ('boolean_features', 'passthrough',
                                 ['HIGHT_BLOOD_PRESURE',
                                  'HAS_HIGHT_CHOLESTEROL',
                                  'HAS_CHECKED_CHOLESTEROL', 'IS_SMOKER',
                                  'HAD_STROKE', 'HAD_HEARTH_ATTACK_OR_DISEASE',
                                  'HAVE_EXERCISED', 'HAD_FRUITS', 'HAD_VEGGIES',
                                  'CONSUME_ALCOOL', 'HAS_HEALTHCARE',
                                  'NO_DOCTOR_APPOINTMENT_BY_COST'])])
